title: <div>  2.  <b>Andrew works harder → Fletcher raises the bar, pushing him past hi
content:
2. Andrew works harder → Fletcher raises the bar, pushing him past his limit.
3. Andrew finally plays well → Fletcher brings in another drummer to replace him.
4. Andrew suffers a breakdown → Fletcher brings him back, then sets him up to fail.
5. Andrew has nothing left → He chooses to fight back, winning through music.

📌 McKee’s Principle Applied:
• If conflict doesn’t escalate, the audience loses interest.
• Every scene must raise the stakes emotionally, physically, or psychologically.

5. The Ending Must Be Both Surprising and Inevitable

McKee argues that the best endings are:
• Surprising (not predictable) → They don’t follow clichés or obvious setups.
• Inevitable (earned through story logic) → Looking back, the ending feels like the only possible resolution.

🔥 How Whiplash Achieves This
• Surprising: Instead of giving up or winning a competition, Andrew defies Fletcher onstage, stealing control of the performance.
context: From document: 1.1.1. | The Story Problem | subheading — chunk 6 (chunk 6/8)
